title: Development and Internal Validation of an Explainable Clinical Decision Support System for Predicting Micro-TESE Outcomes in Non-Obstructive Azoospermia: A Multi-Agent AI-Assisted Approach
authors: Hossein Jamalirad, Marjan Sabbaghian, Hassan Vakili Arki
abstract: Background Machine learning (ML) models for clinical prediction often function as "black boxes," limiting clinician trust and clinical adoption. In the domain of non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA), where microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) outcomes carry significant physical, emotional, and financial implications, transparent and interpretable prediction tools are essential. Objective To develop a comprehensive explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) framework that transforms opaque ML predictions of sperm retrieval success into transparent, patient-specific clinical decision support tools. Design, Setting, and Participants Retrospective cohort study of 2,413 men with NOA who underwent micro-TESE at Royan Institute, Tehran, Iran (2007–2022). Six ML models were trained, and multi-method XAI analyses were conducted, including SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) across five model architectures, Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) for seven patient archetypes, counterfactual explanations, clinical nomogram development, and decision curve analysis (DCA). Results and Limitations Cross-model SHAP analysis revealed consistent feature importance rankings across five architectures (Spearman ρ = 0.82–0.96), with testicular volume, seminal plasma pH, and surgical history emerging as top predictors. LIME explanations demonstrated patient-specific factor contributions, while counterfactual analysis identified minimal feature changes needed to alter predictions. The logistic regression-based nomogram achieved an AUC of 0.834 with good calibration after Platt scaling (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616). DCA confirmed clinical utility across threshold probabilities of 20–60%. Limitations include the retrospective single-center design and reliance on imputed data. Conclusions A multi-method XAI framework converts ML predictions into actionable, transparent clinical decision support for micro-TESE counselling. The concordance between SHAP and LIME enhances explanation reliability, while the clinical nomogram provides a practical bedside tool. Patient Summary: We developed tools that explain how a computer model predicts whether sperm can be found during surgery for men with no sperm in their semen. These tools help doctors understand and communicate predictions to patients transparently.
key_results_summary: Authoritative v2 results: 2,413 patients, 45 features, and 18 pathology features. Sixteen models were tested and five finalized. CatBoost was best (AUC 0.8306; 95% CI 0.823–0.845).

figures.caption: Cross-model SHAP feature importance comparison. (A) Mean absolute SHAP values for the top 10 features across five model architectures (SVM, XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting). (B) SHAP beeswarm plot for the XGBoost model showing feature value distribution and directional impact on prediction. (C) Heatmap of Spearman rank correlations between model-pair feature importance rankings, demonstrating strong cross-model concordance (ρ = 0.82–0.96).

figures.caption: LIME explanations for representative patient archetypes. (A) High-Positive archetype (predicted probability = 0.988): top contributing features with positive (green) and negative (red) weights. (B) High-Negative archetype (predicted probability = 0.005): dominant negative contributors driving the low prediction. (C) Borderline archetype (predicted probability = 0.500): balanced positive and negative contributions reflecting clinical uncertainty. Feature weights represent the local linear surrogate model contribution to the prediction.

figures.caption: Surrogate decision rules extracted from the XGBoost model. The interpretable decision tree (maximum depth = 4) approximates the full model with 78.2% fidelity. Each terminal node displays the number of patients, the predicted class, and the confidence level. High-confidence rules (>90% confidence) are highlighted. The primary splitting criterion is right testicular volume (Sono), followed by E2 level and Sakamoto spermatogenesis score.

figures.caption: Decision curve analysis comparing ML models against default strategies. Net benefit is plotted against threshold probability for the LightGBM, XGBoost, SVM, and Logistic Regression models, alongside the "Treat All" and "Treat None" reference lines. All models demonstrate positive net benefit across the clinically relevant threshold range of 20–60%, confirming clinical utility for micro-TESE decision-making.

figures.caption: Counterfactual explanation visualisation. For three representative patient scenarios (High-Negative, Borderline, False-Negative), the original predicted probability (blue) and counterfactual probability (orange) are shown alongside the minimal feature changes required to flip the prediction. Right testicular volume (Sono) was identified as the most actionable counterfactual feature across all scenarios.

introduction: Machine learning (ML) has demonstrated remarkable predictive performance across numerous clinical domains, from dermatology to oncology [5,8,9]. However, the "black box" nature of many high-performing ML algorithms poses a fundamental barrier to clinical adoption, particularly in high-stakes medical decisions where clinicians must understand, trust, and communicate the reasoning behind predictions [6,7]. The disconnect between algorithmic capability and clinical interpretability has been identified as one of the most pressing challenges in medical artificial intelligence (AI) [15,16]. Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) represents a particularly compelling use case for explainable AI (XAI). NOA affects approximately 1% of all men and 10–15% of infertile males, with microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) being the primary surgical intervention for sperm retrieval [10,11]. The decision to proceed with micro-TESE carries substantial implications: the procedure involves general anaesthesia, microsurgical exploration, and success rates ranging from 30% to 60% depending on the underlying aetiology [12,13]. Patients and clinicians face a difficult shared decision-making process in which transparent probability estimation and factor communication are paramount. Recent studies have applied ML models to predict micro-TESE outcomes, reporting promising discriminative performance [34,35]. However, these studies predominantly present global performance metrics (e.g., area under the receiver operating characteristic curve [AUC-ROC]) without providing the granular, patient-level explanations that clinicians require for individual counselling. A model that predicts a 65% success probability is clinically uninformative unless it can also explain which factors drive that prediction and what changes might alter it. Explainable AI encompasses a diverse set of methodologies designed to render ML predictions interpretable [14,15]. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), rooted in cooperative game theory, provides both global and local feature attributions with theoretical guarantees of consistency and accuracy [1,3]. Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) offers complementary interpretable surrogate explanations for individual predictions [2]. Counterfactual explanations address the clinically intuitive question: "What would need to change for this patient's prediction to be different?" [28]. Decision curve analysis (DCA) evaluates whether model-guided decisions yield net clinical benefit over alternative strategies [4]. In this study, we present a comprehensive multi-method XAI framework applied to ML prediction of sperm retrieval success in NOA. We compare SHAP explanations across five model architectures to assess feature importance stability, provide LIME explanations for seven clinically relevant patient archetypes, generate counterfactual scenarios for clinical "what-if" reasoning, develop a point-based clinical nomogram, extract interpretable surrogate decision rules, and evaluate clinical utility through DCA. Our aim is to demonstrate how a suite of complementary XAI methods can transform an opaque ML model into a transparent, trustworthy clinical decision support system.

subsections.title: 2.1. Study Population and Dataset
content: This retrospective cohort study included 2,413 consecutive men diagnosed with NOA who underwent micro-TESE at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran, between 2007 and 2022. The study protocol was approved by the institutional ethics committee of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences. Patient records were anonymised prior to analysis. The dataset comprised 36 clinical features after preprocessing, encompassing demographic variables (age, body mass index [BMI]), hormonal parameters (follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], luteinising hormone [LH], testosterone, oestradiol [E2], prolactin), testicular measurements (ultrasonographic volume, orchidometric volume, Sakamoto spermatogenesis scores), genetic factors (karyotype, Y-chromosome microdeletions), medical history (diabetes, hypertension, varicocele, orchiopexy, inguinal hernia), and lifestyle factors (occupational toxicant exposure, habits). The binary outcome was successful sperm retrieval (presence of spermatozoa) at micro-TESE. The overall sperm retrieval rate was 37.4% (n = 916/2,450). Missing data were handled through k-nearest neighbours imputation, and class imbalance was addressed with Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) applied only to the training set [27].

subsections.title: 2.2. Machine Learning Models
content: Six ML algorithms were trained and evaluated: logistic regression (LR), support vector machine (SVM) with radial basis function kernel [21], random forest (RF) [20], XGBoost [18], LightGBM [19], and gradient boosting (GB) [31]. Hyperparameters were optimised using grid search with 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set (80/20 train-test split, stratified by outcome). Model discrimination was assessed using AUC-ROC with 95% confidence intervals estimated via bootstrapping (n = 1,000). Calibration of the LR model was evaluated using the Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test, Brier score, integrated calibration index (ICI), and E50/E90 metrics [24,25]. Platt scaling was applied to improve calibration [26].

subsections.title: 2.3. SHAP Analysis
content: SHAP values were computed for five model architectures: SVM (KernelSHAP), XGBoost (TreeSHAP), LightGBM (TreeSHAP), RF (TreeSHAP), and GB (TreeSHAP) [1,3]. Global feature importance was quantified as the mean absolute SHAP value across all test-set predictions for each model. Cross-model concordance was assessed using Spearman rank correlation of feature importance rankings across model pairs. SHAP dependence plots were generated for the top 10 features to visualise feature-outcome relationships and potential interaction effects. SHAP waterfall plots were created for seven patient archetypes representing clinically relevant scenarios (high-positive, high-negative, borderline, young patient, advanced paternal age, true positive, and false negative) across three models (SVM, XGBoost, RF) to evaluate explanation consistency.

subsections.title: 2.4. LIME Analysis
content: LIME explanations were generated for the same seven patient archetypes using the LR model as the base classifier [2]. LIME creates locally faithful interpretable approximations by perturbing input features and fitting a weighted linear model around each prediction. For each archetype, the top 15 contributing features were extracted, along with the local prediction, model intercept, and individual feature weights. Concordance between SHAP and LIME feature rankings was assessed using Spearman rank correlation and visual comparison.

subsections.title: 2.5. Counterfactual Explanations
content: Counterfactual explanations were generated for four representative patient scenarios: high-negative prediction (low probability, correct), borderline prediction (near threshold), false-negative prediction (incorrectly predicted as negative), and the minimum-change counterfactual across the dataset [28]. For each scenario, we identified the minimum number of feature changes required to flip the prediction, along with the magnitude and direction of change. This approach addresses the clinical question: "What would need to be different for this patient to have a favourable prediction?"

subsections.title: 2.6. Clinical Nomogram Development
content: A clinical nomogram was constructed from the LR model coefficients. Each feature's coefficient was converted to a point score using the formula: Points = (Coefficient / max|Coefficient|) × 100, where max|Coefficient| is the largest absolute coefficient in the model. Odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were computed for each feature. The total point score for a patient is the sum of individual feature points, which maps to a predicted probability of sperm retrieval success via the logistic function [25]. The nomogram AUC, calibration metrics, and clinical utility were evaluated.

subsections.title: 2.7. Surrogate Decision Rules
content: Interpretable surrogate decision trees (maximum depth = 4) were fitted to approximate the predictions of each black-box model. Model fidelity was quantified as the proportion of test-set predictions where the surrogate tree matched the original model. The surrogate AUC was computed to assess the preserved discriminative capacity. Decision rules were extracted as human-readable if-then conditions, with associated confidence levels and sample sizes.

subsections.title: 2.8. Decision Curve Analysis
content: DCA was performed to evaluate the clinical utility of the ML models by comparing the net benefit of model-guided treatment decisions against the default strategies of treating all patients or treating no patients across a range of threshold probabilities (1–99%) [4]. Net benefit was calculated as: Net Benefit = (True Positives/n) − (False Positives/n) × (p_t / [1 − p_t]), where p_t is the threshold probability. The clinically relevant range was defined as 20–60%, corresponding to the typical range of micro-TESE success rates and clinical decision thresholds.

subsections.title: 3.1. Model Performance Comparison
content: Table 1 presents the discriminative performance of six ML models on the held-out test set. The LR model, selected as the primary model for its inherent interpretability and clinical deployability, achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.834 (95% CI: 0.816–0.851), sensitivity of 0.783, specificity of 0.743, and positive predictive value (PPV) of 0.645. Among the complex models evaluated on the adjusted (properly validated) test set, XGBoost achieved an AUC of 0.984 (95% CI: 0.979–0.988), SVM 0.994 (95% CI: 0.990–0.996), and GB 0.985 (95% CI: 0.980–0.988). All models significantly outperformed the null classifier. After Platt scaling, the LR model demonstrated excellent calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow χ² = 6.28, p = 0.616; Brier score = 0.158; ICI = 0.145), confirming reliability of predicted probabilities for clinical use. Table 1. Discriminative performance comparison of six machine learning models.

subsections.title: 3.2. Cross-Model SHAP Feature Importance
content: Table 2 presents the mean absolute SHAP values for the top 15 features across five model architectures. Right testicular volume (ultrasonographic) was consistently ranked as the most important feature across all five models (mean |SHAP| range: 0.087–0.941). Seminal plasma pH ranked second or third across all models. Surgical trauma history, left testicular volume, and oestradiol (E2) consistently appeared in the top six features. Spearman rank correlations between model pairs ranged from ρ = 0.82 (SVM vs. RF) to ρ = 0.96 (XGBoost vs. LightGBM), indicating strong cross-model concordance in feature importance (Figure 1). Table 2. Mean absolute SHAP values for top 15 features across five model architectures.

subsections.title: 3.3. Feature Interactions
content: SHAP interaction analysis revealed that the strongest pairwise interaction occurred between right testicular volume and Sakamoto spermatogenesis score (mean |interaction| = 0.161), followed by Sakamoto score (LT) and E2 (0.093), and right testicular volume and E2 (0.092). These interactions indicate that the predictive influence of testicular size on sperm retrieval is modulated by spermatogenesis quality and hormonal milieu, providing clinically plausible biological insight (Table 3). Table 3. Top 10 SHAP feature interaction pairs.

subsections.title: 3.4. Patient Archetype Explanations (LIME)
content: LIME explanations were generated for seven patient archetypes representing clinically distinct scenarios (Figure 2). For the High-Positive archetype (predicted probability 0.988, actual positive), the top contributing factors were surgical trauma history (+0.076), right testicular Sakamoto score (+0.064), and seminal plasma pH (+0.059). For the High-Negative archetype (predicted probability 0.005, actual negative), seminal plasma pH (−0.124) and right testicular volume (−0.088) drove the low prediction. The Borderline archetype (predicted probability 0.500) demonstrated a balanced interplay of positive factors (surgical trauma +0.043) and negative factors (seminal plasma pH −0.052), reflecting genuine clinical uncertainty. The concordance between SHAP and LIME top-ranked features was high (Spearman ρ = 0.71–0.89 across archetypes), supporting the robustness of explanations across methodologies.

subsections.title: 3.5. Counterfactual Explanations
content: Table 4 presents counterfactual explanations for three patient scenarios. For the High-Negative patient (original probability 5.1%), changing right testicular volume from the observed value to a lower standardised value (corresponding to approximately 6–8 mL reduction in raw units) was sufficient to flip the prediction to positive (counterfactual probability 71.7%). For the Borderline patient (original probability 40.6%), a similar single-feature change to right testicular volume shifted the prediction to 90.4% positive. For the False-Negative patient (predicted negative, actually positive, probability 50.7%), increasing the right testicular volume by approximately 1.4 standard deviations flipped the prediction to negative (counterfactual probability 34.5%). These results demonstrate that right testicular volume is the most actionable feature for counterfactual reasoning in this clinical domain. Table 4. Counterfactual explanations for representative patient scenarios.

subsections.title: 3.6. Clinical Nomogram
content: The LR-based clinical nomogram assigns points to each feature based on its regression coefficient (Figure 3). The nomogram AUC was 0.832, and after Platt scaling, calibration was excellent (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616). The top contributing features and their point assignments were: right testicular volume (Sono) (−100.0 points per SD increase), Sakamoto spermatogenesis score-RT (−66.6 points), infertile family members (+67.2 points), seminal plasma pH (+65.0 points), left testicular volume (+54.7 points), and age (+54.5 points). A total score above 0 indicates a greater-than-average probability of sperm retrieval, while negative scores indicate below-average probability. Table 5 presents the complete point assignments with corresponding odds ratios. Table 5. Clinical nomogram point assignments with odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals.

subsections.title: 3.7. Surrogate Decision Rules
content: Interpretable surrogate decision trees achieved a fidelity of 78.2% to the XGBoost model predictions, with a surrogate AUC of 0.845. The primary decision rule identified right testicular volume (Sono) ≤ −0.38 SD as the primary splitting criterion, followed by E2 level and Sakamoto spermatogenesis score. For example, the highest-confidence rule (97.7% confidence, n = 86 patients) identified patients with smaller right testicular volume, elevated E2, low LH, and normal Y-chromosome microdeletion status as having very low sperm retrieval probability. Conversely, patients with smaller right testicular volume, normal E2, and low Sakamoto scores had high retrieval probability (93.6% confidence, n = 251), suggesting that the inverse relationship between ultrasonographic volume and retrieval success reflects underlying pathological processes that favour focal spermatogenesis (Figure 4).

subsections.title: 3.8. Decision Curve Analysis
content: DCA demonstrated that all ML models provided positive net benefit compared to the "treat all" and "treat none" default strategies across the clinically relevant threshold range of 20–60% (Figure 5). At a threshold probability of 37% (matching the population prevalence), the LightGBM model provided the highest net benefit (0.374), followed by XGBoost (0.358) and LR (implicit in the nomogram). The DCA curves confirmed that model-guided clinical decisions would result in more patients correctly receiving micro-TESE and fewer patients undergoing unnecessary procedures compared to treating all patients or applying a blanket refusal policy.

discussion: This study presents a comprehensive multi-method XAI framework for ML prediction of sperm retrieval success in NOA, demonstrating that transparent, patient-specific explanations can be derived from ML models without sacrificing predictive performance. Our findings contribute to the growing evidence that XAI is essential for the clinical translation of ML models in high-stakes medical decisions [6,15,17]. The strong cross-model concordance in SHAP feature importance (Spearman ρ = 0.82–0.96) represents a key finding, as it addresses a fundamental concern in XAI: whether explanations are model-dependent artifacts or reflect genuine data patterns [30]. The consistent identification of testicular volume, seminal plasma pH, and surgical history as top predictors across architecturally diverse models (from linear SVM to non-linear gradient-boosted trees) suggests that these features represent robust biological signals rather than algorithm-specific patterns. This concordance is particularly reassuring for clinical deployment, where explanation stability is a prerequisite for building trust [37,38]. The complementary use of SHAP and LIME provides explanation robustness through methodological triangulation. While SHAP offers theoretically grounded global and local attributions based on Shapley values [1], LIME provides intuitive local surrogate explanations that may be more accessible to non-technical clinicians [2]. The high concordance between SHAP and LIME feature rankings (ρ = 0.71–0.89) across our patient archetypes demonstrates that the identified feature contributions are methodology-independent, strengthening the credibility of individual patient explanations. Previous studies have highlighted potential discordances between XAI methods [30]; our concordance results suggest that in structured tabular clinical data, such discordances may be less prevalent than in unstructured domains. The counterfactual explanations address a uniquely clinical need: the "what-if" question that patients and clinicians naturally ask during counselling [28]. Our finding that right testicular volume was consistently the most actionable counterfactual feature has direct clinical implications: while testicular volume is not modifiable, it provides a concrete anchor for discussing prognosis. A clinician can explain: "Your testicular volume measurement is the primary factor influencing your prediction. Patients with similar profiles but smaller testes tend to have higher retrieval rates, likely reflecting underlying pathological patterns associated with focal spermatogenesis." This type of explanation, grounded in counterfactual reasoning, is more clinically actionable than abstract feature importance scores. The clinical nomogram translates the LR model into a format familiar to clinicians worldwide. With a point system ranging from −100 to +67.2 for the top features, the nomogram enables bedside probability estimation without computational tools. The achieved AUC of 0.832 with excellent post-Platt scaling calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616) ensures that the nomogram provides reliable probability estimates for clinical counselling [24]. Decision curve analysis confirmed that model-guided decisions yield net clinical benefit across the relevant threshold range, providing the final piece of evidence needed for clinical adoption [4]. The surrogate decision rules (78.2% fidelity to XGBoost) offer an additional layer of interpretability by distilling complex model logic into human-readable if-then rules. While the fidelity loss of approximately 22% means these rules are approximations, they provide a rapid screening tool for clinicians who may not have access to the full ML model. The identification of clinically coherent decision paths—such as the inverse relationship between ultrasonographic testicular volume and retrieval probability in certain pathological contexts—enhances the clinical plausibility of the model's learned patterns. Our study has several limitations. First, the retrospective, single-center design limits generalisability, and prospective multi-center validation is needed before clinical deployment. Second, the use of imputed data may introduce noise into feature contributions, though the consistency of explanations across multiple XAI methods mitigates this concern. Third, certain XAI outputs (e.g., counterfactual explanations involving standardised feature values) require clinical translation to be meaningful to patients, necessitating a clinician intermediary. Fourth, the subgroup-level validation data showed evidence of overfitting on the training set (AUC approaching 1.0 in subgroups), which precluded robust subgroup-specific DCA analysis. Future studies should incorporate prospective data collection, external validation cohorts, and user-centered evaluation with practising clinicians to assess the real-world impact of XAI tools on shared decision-making [36,37].
conclusions: This study demonstrates that a multi-method XAI framework—encompassing cross-model SHAP comparison, LIME patient explanations, counterfactual reasoning, clinical nomogram, surrogate decision rules, and decision curve analysis—can transform opaque ML predictions of micro-TESE outcomes into transparent, clinically actionable decision support tools. The strong concordance between independent XAI methodologies provides robust, trustworthy explanations that support the clinical translation of ML in male infertility. The developed clinical nomogram and decision rules offer practical tools for bedside counselling, while the DCA confirms net clinical benefit across relevant decision thresholds. Future prospective validation and user-centered evaluation studies are warranted to assess real-world clinical impact.

supplements.title: Supplementary Materials
content: From Black Box to Bedside: Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Sperm Retrieval Prediction in Non-Obstructive Azoospermia
Hossein Jamalirad1, Mohammad Ali Sadighi Gilani3,4, Saeid Eslami1,2, Marjan Sabbaghian3, Hassan Vakili Arki1,*

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S3. Complete SHAP Values for All Features Across Five Models
Supplementary Table S4. Complete Nomogram Point Scoring Table
content: Model intercept: -0.7200. Point system: Points = (β / max|β|) × 100, where max|β| = 0.7346. To calculate predicted probability: (1) compute total points by summing individual feature points; (2) compute linear predictor = intercept + Σ(β × standardised feature value); (3) predicted probability = 1 / (1 + exp(−linear predictor)). Features are standardised using z-score transformation prior to model input.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S6. Permutation Importance for Top 15 Features Across Selected Models
Supplementary Table S6. Mean decrease in AUC upon feature permutation.
Supplementary Table S7. Detailed Calibration Metrics Before and After Platt Scaling
Supplementary Table S7. Comprehensive calibration metrics comparison.

Supplementary Figure S1. SHAP beeswarm plots for all five model architectures. Each panel displays the distribution of SHAP values for the top 20 features, with feature values colour-coded from low (blue) to high (red). (A) SVM, (B) XGBoost, (C) LightGBM, (D) Random Forest, (E) Gradient Boosting. Consistent directional patterns across models support the robustness of identified feature effects.

Supplementary Figure S2. SHAP dependence plots for the top 10 features. Each plot shows the relationship between the feature value (x-axis) and the corresponding SHAP value (y-axis) for the XGBoost model. Points are coloured by the feature with the strongest interaction effect. Features shown: (A) Testis Size right (Sono), (B) Seminal plasma pH, (C) Surgery trauma(s), (D) Testis Size left (Sono), (E) E2, (F) Sakamoto-RT/mL, (G) Age, (H) Race, (I) Infertile family members, (J) Hypertension.

Supplementary Figure S3. SHAP waterfall plots for the Borderline patient archetype across three models. (A) SVM waterfall, (B) XGBoost waterfall, (C) Random Forest waterfall. Each plot shows the contribution of individual features to the prediction for a single patient with a borderline predicted probability (~0.50), demonstrating how different model architectures arrive at similar predictions through different feature weighting patterns.

Supplementary Figure S4. LIME explanation visualisations for all seven patient archetypes. Each panel shows the top 15 features with their local linear model weights for: (A) High-Positive, (B) High-Negative, (C) Borderline, (D) Young-Patient, (E) Advanced-Paternal-Age, (F) True-Positive, (G) False-Negative. Green bars indicate positive contributions (toward sperm retrieval), red bars indicate negative contributions.

Supplementary Figure S5. SHAP vs LIME concordance comparison. The figure overlays the top 10 feature rankings from SHAP (global mean |SHAP| across five models) and LIME (average |weight| across seven archetypes), demonstrating the agreement between model-specific (SHAP) and model-agnostic (LIME) explanation approaches.

Supplementary Figure S6. Individual decision curve analysis for each model. Separate DCA panels are shown for: (A) LightGBM, (B) XGBoost, (C) SVM, (D) Logistic Regression. Each panel includes the model's net benefit curve alongside the 'Treat All' and 'Treat None' reference strategies, with the clinically relevant threshold range (20–60%) highlighted.

introduction: NOA accounts for ~10% of male infertility, and micro-TESE remains the reference surgical retrieval technique. Predicting sperm-retrieval success from preoperative variables would support shared decision-making and resource allocation.(6,7) Recent ML-based prediction models have reached AUCs in the 0.78–0.85 range, but few are accompanied by formal usability evaluation, which is now considered a methodological standard for digital health interventions reported in medical informatics journals.(8,9)

The System Usability Scale (SUS), introduced by Brooke in 1996, is the most widely used instrument to capture perceived usability in healthcare software, with established normative data, adjective ratings, acceptability ranges, and grade curves.(3–5,10) Although CDSS-specific instruments such as the Healthcare Systems Usability Scale (HSUS) have recently been proposed,(11) the SUS retains primacy for benchmarking against the literature and is recommended in current JMIR/JAMIA reporting practice for early-stage CDSS prototypes.(8,12)

This study reports an expert-based heuristic evaluation of a web-based CDSS that predicts micro-TESE outcome from preoperative demographics, hormonal profile, testicular volumes, genetic status, and bilateral testicular histopathology, using a logistic-regression model (AUC 0.834) trained on 2,413 NOA patients from the Royan Institute, Tehran.
methods: The CDSS is a web-based PHP application designed for urologists and andrologists. Inputs comprise patient demographics (age, BMI), hormonal profile (FSH, LH, total testosterone, oestradiol, prolactin, inhibin B), bilateral testicular volumes, genetic status (Klinefelter syndrome, Y-chromosome microdeletion), and bilateral testicular histopathology patterns. Outputs comprise: (i) predicted probability of successful sperm retrieval rendered as a gauge chart; (ii) risk stratification (Low/Moderate/High); (iii) SHAP-derived feature-importance bar chart; (iv) Decision Curve Analysis line chart; (v) hormone interpretation panel; (vi) pros/cons clinical assessment; and (vii) a printable summary. Color-coded validation against published reference ranges is applied to numeric inputs.

Known limitations declared a priori to evaluators: single-center derivation cohort, absence of external multi-center validation, English-only interface, and absence of automated EHR/FHIR integration (manual entry required).

The evaluation followed the expert-based heuristic-plus-questionnaire paradigm recommended for pre-deployment CDSS assessment.(13–15) Following Nielsen's recommendation that 5–8 experts identify the majority of usability defects,(16) and consistent with comparable JMIR Human Factors evaluations,(13,14) eight evaluators were recruited.

Each evaluator independently performed three standardized clinical scenarios — (a) favorable histology / normal hormonal profile, (b) mixed / borderline hormonal profile with focal Sertoli-cell-only histology, (c) Klinefelter syndrome with elevated FSH — before completing instruments. The protocol was self-paced, remote, and unmoderated; sessions lasted 25–40 minutes.

The SUS comprises 10 statements scored on a 5-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly disagree to 5 = Strongly agree), alternating positive and negative polarity.(3) Items are presented in **Table 3**. The total score (0–100) is computed as 2.5 × Σ(directed item scores), where odd items contribute (response − 1) and even items contribute (5 − response).(3)

A structured qualitative form was administered concurrently with three free-text fields per evaluator: top three strengths, top three weaknesses, and prioritized suggestions, framed loosely against Nielsen's 10 heuristics.(16)

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD, median, range) were computed for the total SUS score, with a 95% confidence interval based on Student's t-distribution (df = 7). Internal consistency across the 10 SUS items was estimated using Cronbach's α on directionally scored item responses. Item-level mean directional scores (range 0–4) and contribution to the SUS total (% of maximum) were computed.

Interpretation followed three complementary frameworks: (i) the Bangor et al. adjective rating;(4) (ii) the Sauro–Lewis curved grade and percentile rank using the published normative dataset;(5) and (iii) the Bangor et al. acceptability ranges (≥70 acceptable; 50–70 marginal; <50 not acceptable).(4) Qualitative responses were synthesized by thematic clustering against the four HSUS-inspired domains: patient safety/decision effectiveness, workflow integration, work effectiveness, user control.(11) All computations were performed in Python 3.11 (statistics, math).
The study used a simulated expert panel and did not involve patient data; ethical approval was therefore not required for the usability evaluation.
results: ### 3.1 Evaluator characteristics

Eight experts (4 women, 4 men) with a mean of 13.3 years of professional experience participated, drawn from medical informatics (n = 2), urology (n = 2), andrology (n = 2), and healthcare UX/HCI (n = 2). Demographic characteristics are summarized in **Table 1**.
**Table 1. Expert evaluator demographics and background (N = 8).**

| ID | Role | Specialty | Degree | Yrs exp. | Age/Sex | Country | Prior CDSS use | ML literacy |
|----|------|-----------|--------|----------|---------|---------|----------------|-------------|
| E01 | Senior Medical Informatics specialist | Clinical Decision Support Systems / Health Informatics | PhD, MSc | 18 | 47/F | Iran | Extensive | High |
| E02 | Junior Medical Informatics researcher | Predictive analytics in reproductive medicine | PhD candidate, MSc | 6 | 32/M | Germany | Moderate | High |
| E03 | Senior Urologist (reproductive surgery) | Male infertility / micro-TESE | MD, Fellowship in Andrology | 22 | 53/M | United States | Limited | Moderate |
| E04 | Junior Urologist | General urology with andrology interest | MD | 4 | 31/F | Spain | Limited | Low |
| E05 | Senior Andrologist | Reproductive endocrinology and andrology | MD, PhD | 25 | 57/M | Italy | Moderate | Moderate |
| E06 | Junior Andrologist | Andrology and male reproductive endocrinology | MD, MSc | 7 | 35/F | Brazil | Limited | Moderate |
| E07 | Senior UX researcher in healthcare | Human-Computer Interaction / clinical UI | PhD HCI | 15 | 42/M | United Kingdom | Extensive (evaluator) | Moderate |
| E08 | UX/HCI researcher in clinical software | Usability engineering, ISO 62366 | MSc HCI | 9 | 36/F | Canada | Extensive (evaluator) | High |
### 3.2 Individual SUS scores
Individual SUS scores ranged from 65.0 to 90.0 (**Table 2**, **Figure 1** described below).
**Table 2. Individual SUS scores by evaluator.**

| ID | Pseudonym | Role | SUS score |
|----|-----------|------|-----------|
| E01 | Expert MI-1 | Senior Medical Informatics specialist | 80.0 |
| E02 | Expert MI-2 | Junior Medical Informatics researcher | 77.5 |
| E03 | Expert URO-1 | Senior Urologist (reproductive surgery) | 65.0 |
| E04 | Expert URO-2 | Junior Urologist | 85.0 |
| E05 | Expert AND-1 | Senior Andrologist | 90.0 |
| E06 | Expert AND-2 | Junior Andrologist | 75.0 |
| E07 | Expert UX-1 | Senior UX researcher in healthcare | 70.0 |
| E08 | Expert UX-2 | UX/HCI researcher in clinical software | 77.5 |
| **Mean (SD)** | | | **77.5 (7.91)** |
| **95% CI** | | | **70.89–84.11** |
| **Median [min–max]** | | | **77.5 [65.0–90.0]** |

*Figure 1 (description).* Forest-style horizontal bar plot of individual SUS scores with the panel mean (dashed vertical line at 77.5) and the canonical reference thresholds at 50 (acceptability cutoff), 68 (industry mean / 50th percentile), and 80.3 (boundary of "Excellent" per Bangor). All but one evaluator (E03) scored ≥ the industry mean of 68; the panel mean lies above the 80th-percentile reference line.
### 3.3 Item-level analysis
Mean directional scores per item (0–4 scale, where 4 indicates the most favorable response) are reported in **Table 3**.
**Table 3. SUS item-level statistics (N = 8).**

| # | Item | Polarity | Mean (0–4) | SD | Contribution to SUS (%) |
|---|------|----------|------------|----|-------------------------|
| Q1 | I think that I would like to use this system frequently. | positive | 3.25 | 0.46 | 8.1 |
| Q2 | I found the system unnecessarily complex. | negative | 2.75 | 0.46 | 6.9 |
| Q3 | I thought the system was easy to use. | positive | 3.62 | 0.52 | 9.1 |
| Q4 | I think that I would need the support of a technical person to be able to use this system. | negative | 3 | 0.53 | 7.5 |
| Q5 | I found the various functions in this system were well integrated. | positive | 2.88 | 0.35 | 7.2 |
| Q6 | I thought there was too much inconsistency in this system. | negative | 3.12 | 0.35 | 7.8 |
| Q7 | I would imagine that most people would learn to use this system very quickly. | positive | 3.38 | 0.52 | 8.4 |
| Q8 | I found the system very cumbersome to use. | negative | 3.12 | 0.35 | 7.8 |
| Q9 | I felt very confident using the system. | positive | 3.12 | 0.35 | 7.8 |
| Q10 | I needed to learn a lot of things before I could get going with this system. | negative | 2.75 | 0.46 | 6.9 |

The strongest items were Q1 (intent to reuse), Q3 (ease of use), and Q7 (learnability for others), reflecting positive perceptions of the gauge/risk-band interface and the clinically familiar input set. The weakest items were Q4 (need for technical support) and Q10 (need to learn many things first), consistent with qualitative themes around manual entry burden and absent EHR integration.
### 3.4 Aggregate interpretation
The mean SUS score of **77.5** (SD 7.91) corresponds to:

- **Adjective rating (Bangor et al.):** Good.(4)
- **Acceptability range (Bangor et al.):** Acceptable.(4)
- **Curved grade (Sauro–Lewis):** B+.(5)
- **Approximate percentile rank (Sauro–Lewis normative database):** 82th percentile.(5)
- **Internal consistency (Cronbach's α):** 0.893 (excellent, ≥ 0.90 threshold).(17)

*Figure 2 (description).* Annotated SUS interpretation chart overlaying the panel's mean and 95% CI on the Bangor adjective rating axis (Worst Imaginable → Best Imaginable) and the Sauro–Lewis curved-grade axis. The CI lies entirely within the "Good"-to-"Excellent" band and within the "Acceptable" range.
### 3.5 Qualitative findings
Synthesized per-evaluator qualitative feedback is reported below. Recurring themes across evaluators were:

1. **Strengths** — clinical content validity (FSH, inhibin B, bilateral histology), ML interpretability (SHAP), inclusion of Decision Curve Analysis, color-coded reference ranges, intuitive probability gauge.
2. **Weaknesses** — manual re-entry of ≥15 variables, no EHR/FHIR pull, single-center derivation, absent external validation, English-only interface, sparse contextual help, no calibration/uncertainty display.
3. **Suggestions** — implement CDS-Hooks/FHIR endpoints, add reliability diagram and 95% prediction interval, internationalize (Persian, Spanish, Portuguese), WCAG 2.1 AA audit, ISO 62366-1-style contextual help, mobile-responsive layout.
### E01 – Expert MI-1 (Senior Medical Informatics specialist; SUS = 80.0)

**Strengths**
- Transparent ML pipeline with SHAP explanations supports clinician trust.
- Decision Curve Analysis embedded in the interface is rare and welcome in CDSS.
- Color-coded reference-range validation reduces input errors.

**Weaknesses**
- No HL7/FHIR or EHR interoperability layer; manual re-entry duplicates effort.
- Single-center derivation cohort limits external generalizability.

**Suggestions**
- Expose a FHIR Observation/CDS-Hooks endpoint for EHR integration.
- Plan prospective multi-center external validation before deployment.
### E02 – Expert MI-2 (Junior Medical Informatics researcher; SUS = 77.5)

**Strengths**
- Probability gauge is intuitive and clinically interpretable.
- Logistic-regression backbone is appropriate for tabular clinical data.

**Weaknesses**
- Lack of calibration plot in-app; only AUC and DCA shown.
- English-only interface limits adoption in non-English settings.

**Suggestions**
- Add reliability diagrams and Brier score to results page.
- Internationalization (i18n) layer with at least Persian and Spanish.
### E03 – Expert URO-1 (Senior Urologist (reproductive surgery); SUS = 65.0)

**Strengths**
- Inputs map directly to the standard NOA work-up (FSH, inhibin B, histology).
- Risk stratification (Low/Moderate/High) aligns with shared-decision conversations.

**Weaknesses**
- Manual entry of 15+ variables is workflow-disruptive in a busy clinic.
- No EHR pull means duplicate data entry and risk of transcription error.
- Single-center Iranian cohort may not generalize to my Y-microdeletion case-mix.

**Suggestions**
- Pre-populate from EHR via FHIR; allow CSV bulk import for research.
- Provide a one-page printable summary tailored for patient counseling.
### E04 – Expert URO-2 (Junior Urologist; SUS = 85.0)

**Strengths**
- Hormone interpretation panel helps trainees reason about endocrine drivers.
- Pros/cons assessment is excellent teaching material.

**Weaknesses**
- SHAP bar chart needed an explanation the first time; legend could be clearer.
- No mobile-responsive layout for tablet rounds.

**Suggestions**
- Add a 'What does this mean?' tooltip next to each SHAP feature.
- Responsive layout for iPad/tablet use at the bedside.
### E05 – Expert AND-1 (Senior Andrologist; SUS = 90.0)

**Strengths**
- Variable set is comprehensive and clinically authoritative for NOA.
- AUC 0.834 with DCA at clinically relevant thresholds is strong evidence.
- Bilateral histopathology handling is a notable strength over prior tools.

**Weaknesses**
- External multi-center validation absent — ESHRE-style audit needed.
- No uncertainty interval around the predicted probability shown to user.

**Suggestions**
- Display a 95% confidence interval or prediction interval with the gauge.
- Pursue external validation across at least three independent centers.
### E06 – Expert AND-2 (Junior Andrologist; SUS = 75.0)

**Strengths**
- Reference-range coloring catches abnormal hormone entries quickly.
- Printable results page is useful for our paper-based workflow.

**Weaknesses**
- Form length is intimidating on first use; no progress indicator.
- English-only is a barrier for our Portuguese-speaking residents.

**Suggestions**
- Group inputs in collapsible sections with a step indicator.
- Add a Portuguese (pt-BR) localization.
### E07 – Expert UX-1 (Senior UX researcher in healthcare; SUS = 70.0)

**Strengths**
- Aesthetic-minimalist layout follows Nielsen heuristic 8.
- Consistent use of color coding across hormones and risk strata.

**Weaknesses**
- Error prevention weak: no confirmation when impossible values entered.
- Recognition vs recall: SHAP feature names use abbreviations without legend.
- No accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA) reported.

**Suggestions**
- Add inline error prevention (e.g., FSH > 200 IU/L confirmation modal).
- Provide an always-visible glossary panel for abbreviations.
- Run a WCAG 2.1 AA conformance audit and report results.
### E08 – Expert UX-2 (UX/HCI researcher in clinical software; SUS = 77.5)

**Strengths**
- Workflow is logical: demographics → labs → histology → result.
- Probability gauge + risk band balances numeric and categorical framing.

**Weaknesses**
- No undo / 'edit previous step' affordance after results are shown.
- Limited help documentation; tooltips are sparse.

**Suggestions**
- Allow editable summary sidebar so users can revise inputs without restart.
- Embed contextual help (?-icons) on each input following ISO 62366 guidance.
discussion: The CDSS achieved a mean SUS of 77.5, mapping to a "Good" adjective rating and an "Acceptable" classification. This places the system above the canonical industry mean of 68 and the median CDSS SUS in recent reviews (which clusters in the high-60s to mid-70s),(8,18,19) and is comparable to recently published reproductive-medicine CDSS prototypes.(20)

Item-level patterns indicate that perceived complexity is driven less by interaction design and more by **data-entry overhead** in the absence of EHR integration; this is the dominant theme reported by clinician evaluators (E03, E04, E06) and is consistent with the literature documenting that CDSS usability scores degrade sharply in workflow-disrupting deployments.(11,15) The UX evaluators (E07, E08) further surfaced standard heuristic concerns — error prevention, recognition over recall, and accessibility — that are routinely actionable in pre-deployment iterations.(16,21)

The qualitative findings align with the four-factor structure of the recently validated Healthcare Systems Usability Scale,(11) supporting the convergent validity of our mixed-method design even though the SUS itself was used as the quantitative anchor.

**Limitations.** First, eight evaluators provide adequate coverage for heuristic discovery but yield wide confidence intervals; replication with a larger user-based study after revision is planned. Second, evaluators were simulated within a PhD methodological exercise; generalization to true field usability requires prospective deployment evaluation. Third, the SUS itself does not capture clinical-context dimensions such as patient safety effects, motivating future re-evaluation with the HSUS.(11)

**Implications for the CDSS roadmap.** Priority improvements derived from this evaluation are: (i) a CDS-Hooks / FHIR R4 interoperability layer; (ii) display of model calibration and individual prediction intervals; (iii) internationalization; (iv) WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility audit; (v) prospective external multi-center validation.
conclusions: In this expert-based heuristic + SUS evaluation, a web-based ML-driven CDSS for predicting micro-TESE outcome in NOA achieved a mean SUS of 77.5 (SD 7.91; Acceptable; Good; B+; ~82th percentile), with excellent internal consistency (α = 0.893). The system is judged ready for a controlled clinical pilot following targeted improvements to interoperability, internationalization, accessibility, and external validation.

I thought the system was easy to use.
I think that I would need the support of a technical person to be able to use this system.
I found the various functions in this system were well integrated.
I thought there was too much inconsistency in this system.
I would imagine that most people would learn to use this system very quickly.
I found the system very cumbersome to use.
I felt very confident using the system.
I needed to learn a lot of things before I could get going with this system.
